title: 2. Project Organization > 3.3 Project Risk
text_content: Refer to D23997 Halcyon v2.0 Design and Development Plan for program risk. Specially, the following main risks will impact manufacturing activity: • Design freeze may delay due to technical risk. This design freeze delay will impact mfg activities. Such as preproduction, production start time, and the time for shipment. • Due to BJ factory is warming up for Halcyon v1 production, the resource from BJ factory could be some risk. Mfg NPI PM will closely work with BJ team to make sure we have enough resource for HALv2. • During H2.0 NPI phase, project need borrow H1.0 production for prototype build. The procedure of borrow and return need to be aligned with factory process. • Material purchasing time and lead time will be risk as project only last for about 1 year and only one round for prototype build.

source: D68685 HAL 4.0 Cybersecurity Statement Rev 01.docx.pdf
title: 3.0 Definitions > 4.4 Accountability
text_content: To ensure that the actions performed within the application software of the medical device are traced and a high level of auditability is maintained, Varian devices with application software implement a common infrastructure for user rights and logging. The user and service personnel actions, application states, and data conversions are recorded in a secured, auditable format. Appropriate identifiers such as Patient ID and other object IDs are included to ensure that authorized individuals can determine the users accountable and trace the impacted patient data/information. System administration operations, performed by Varian service personnel are also recorded in a secured, auditable format. Lastly, remote access sessions for the customers using Varian SmartConnect™ Solution is recorded in a secured, non-editable, auditable format for each occurrence of remote access, capturing the user accessing the system and the performed actions.

text_content: Mitigations through product design and labeling are in place. The mitigations were derived from thorough risk analysis processes through which risk control measures are formulated, implemented, and validated during product development. The risk control measures have been tested through verification and validation activities, and through product usability studies. The risks and their associated potential hazards have been assessed via analysis of available clinical data regarding the use of HAL. The safety findings in the clinical data were analyzed in context of the benefits to the patients as described above. Clinical data was analyzed for any evidence of the residual risks of HAL 4.0 identified in the RMR report. No adverse events or clinical toxicities were reported as attributable to the residual risks outlined in Section 5.1.4 (e.g. radiation over/under exposure, collision).

title: 1.0 Introduction > 1.2 Scope
text_content: This Clinical Evaluation Plan covers HAL treatment platform, version 4.0. Clinical evaluation is an ongoing process conducted throughout the life cycle of a medical device. It is first performed during the conformity assessment process leading to the marketing of a medical device and then repeated periodically in the post- marketing setting as new clinical safety and performance information about the device is obtained from surveillance programs and other sources. The clinical evaluation report and the clinical data on which it is based serve as the clinical evidence that supports the general safety and performance requirements for the device. The clinical evaluation report is part of the technical documentation of the device. text_content: This RMR applies to both models of HAL 4.0 and assumes both models unless specifically identified. Models included in the HAL 4.0 Treatment Delivery Platform • Halcyon • Ethos radiotherapy system HAL 4.0 is a new version of the former medical device HAL 3.1 and its predicate devices, therefore this risk management report carries forward the hazards and their causes identified in previous design versions, and any field experiences made during their lifetime in addition to the new hazards introduced by new features introduced in HAL 4.0. This RMR documents the results of the specific activities and processes undertaken to ensure the ongoing safety of this device throughout the product life-cycle. Potential effects of device hazards may impact manufacturing personnel, patients, operators, service personnel, and the environment in which the device is used (including interactions with other 3rd party equipment). All of these elements have been evaluated as part of the risk assessment.

text_content: During the design and development phases of the lifecycle for the application software within Varian medical devices, the integrity of the system build is ensured by controlling the editing process and requiring regular reviews of the code. This is done to ensure that the integrity of the application software is maintained through the construction phase. W hen the software package is ready for release to the user, Varian manufacturing procedures (DND1902) are used to prevent improper or incompatible versions of the software from being distributed or installed. These procedures include measures to ensure the consistency of data, the absence of viruses or malware, and the inclusion of needed user documentation and labeling. For Varian medical devices, installation procedures and updates are handled by trained Varian Service personnel or local Service representatives. The documented test process for validation consists of the Test Design Strategy and modular test cases traced to User Needs and acceptance criterion. Selected test cases may trace to more than one user need according to the clinical workflow. text_content: Appendix A lists all the FMEA items that are linked to Risks from the RMR report and all “Approved” FMEA items that have been analyzed for safety and reliability considerations. Description of Column Headings: DFEMA ID is the unique identifier for the failure. Subsystems is the subsystem that identified the failure mode. Group is the area within the subsystem the failure mode was identified. Context (Component) is a text description of the failing component or assembly. Detailed Context (Function) is a text description of the component’s role/purpose in the subsystem. Part Number is the unique identifier of the component (part or assembly) in the design and manufacturing documentation. Failure Mode is the text description of “how” the component or assembly fails. This is specific manner or way by which a failure occurs in terms of failure of the item (being a part or (sub) system) function under investigation; it may generally describe the way the failure occurs.

text_content: Subsystem control software is responsible for reporting its own version and all versions of its software components. On top of that, the Nodes report their subnodes software version (if any) and versions of all FPGA of the node; as well as their PCB part identifier, dash number, revision and serial number to the SPV. When receiving a workstation read version command message via the command channel, the SPV collects its own versions and all versions reported by all Nodes, formats them in XML and sends them back to the workstation. All version messages are formatted to major.minor.revision.build. A major change indicates significant new or updated system capabilities; there is no guarantee of backward compatibility of hardware or software. If a minor changes, there are feature changes and interface changes, and software compatibility could be impacted. A revision is often used for maintenance releases that introduces minor update or bug fixes,

title: 4.0 Requirements for Risk Management Activities and their Review (Continued)
text_content: This analysis investigates all the aspect of the device from a bottom-up perspective. It identifies any inconsistency, design flaws and potential implementation failures. This FMEA concentrates on the investigation of: a) the architecture solution, its stability, adequateness, and flexibility b) the design and any components, its complexity, reliability, performance, effectiveness, criticality, and its implementation c) any interfaces within the components and 3rd-party applications d) any SOUP components e) Packaging, transportation and storage During the Analysis phase, if it is determined that a failure of a component / sub-system could also result in risk, an additional scenario is added to the RMR. This may result in additional hazard identification where top-down analysis did not identify potential hazards, but in most cases isolates additional contributory factors for existing hazards.

text_content: As part of Validation TRR (merge Quote to Cash (Q2C) activities), Product Life Cycle Services (PLCS) will work with Varian Installation Team to perform validation testing of the HAL v4.0 associated Service Technical Instructions (STI’s).  The STI’s are the Installation and Service documentation required to install, upgrade, and maintain the new SW and HW for the device under test. PLCS will work closely with the Installation Team and Global Customer Support to identify candidates to participate in the validation testing of the installation and upgrade documentation. The clinical evaluation may be based on clinical data relating to a device for which equivalence to the device in question can be demonstrated. title: 1.0 Executive Summary > 1.2 Device Classification > 1.3.6 Operator Profiles (Continued)
text_content: The hospital technician, typically a biomedical engineer, is a local, in-clinic resource that is responsible for maintaining the treatment delivery system and associated software and hardware. This resource is trained to a defined level of competency and is provided with limited-service access to the system. The role of hospital technician is to maintain operation of the treatment delivery system and is the first line of support before contacting the Varian Customer Support Representative and /or will work with the Varian Customer Support Representative to help resolve hardware issues. The system is an assortment of components which can be a combination of hardware and software. When preparing hardware for clinical use, the hospital technician may calibrate the hardware and ensure the accuracy of the hardware parameters. Americas: Physicist, Dosimetrist, Radiation Therapist APAC: Physicist, Radiation Physicist, Medical Physicist EMEA: Physicist

title: 7.0 Verification Activities > 7.1 Risk control measures
text_content: The three risk control options shall be considered in their order of priority: o Inherently safe design and manufacture; o Protective measures in the device itself or in the manufacturing process; o Information for safety (warnings/precautions/contra-indications) and, where appropriate, training to users. All recognized risk control measures shall be implemented to reduce risk to or maintain risk at an acceptable level. Implementation of risk control measures shall continue until successive risk control options or measures do not further reduce the estimated risk. Whenever possible, requirements of international standards or their corresponding harmonized versions shall be utilized as risk control measures. These applied standards are listed or referenced in Appendix B of this RMP. The implementation of risk control measures shall be verified. The effectiveness of the implemented risk control measures shall be verified or, as appropriate, validated.

text_content: The extent of the V&V activities depends upon the type of test. Three different types are defined below: Module Test: Software Definition:A software module cannot be used on its own by the clinical operator. Software modules are integrated into higher level user applications/devices. Examples of software modules are patient explorer, OSP, drivers, and service analysis and deployment tools. Test Scope: Software modules which meet the definition above need to be qualified by verification tests only. There is no need to perform validation test because the validation is performed on the application/device where the component is integrated. However, testing of modules in the integrated environment may be useful. Integration Test: (Standalone Applications/Devices, Accessories) Definition:Integration Testing is the testing of standalone devices/applications and their accessories. Standalone applications/devices and specific accessories are complete devices with their own intended use.

text_content: Verification Testing Halcyon SN4 was built per Pre-production Phase I (Silver) DMR, and is maintained in the production equivalent configuration using Discrepancy Report (DR) Handling and Engineering Change (EC) and QNotes processes. Issues that are found during testing are logged as DRs, and the impact to verification and validation testing is assessed. This assessment defines whether the part needs to be replaced or reworked in the machines after implementation of the design change. This decision is documented in the DR. The hardware changes are implemented, and the final material dispositioning (per DR) is done via EC process. Upgrades are performed per the information in ECs and DR and are documented in QNotes system. The Device Parts List of the verification devices is captured at the time of build, and before verification TRR. Validation Testing Halcyon SN4 was upgraded to the final production equivalent configuration (defined by Gold DMR).

Maintenance Plan Varian implements a product lifecycle framework that guides the incorporation of the Secure by Design principle in Varian products and solutions. The lifecycle framework also addresses the continuous management of cybersecurity risks after the products or solutions are released to the field. As part of the post-market cybersecurity maintenance, cybersecurity risk assessments are conducted periodically, or whenever it is needed in response to post-market cybersecurity signals such as 3rd party component vulnerability disclosure (e.g., critical Microsoft vulnerability CVE-2019-0708). Depending on nature and the level of risks, the results of the post-market cybersecurity risk assessments can lead to product updates, bug fixes, field services, Product Advisory, or Product Security Notification.

text_content: For test execution after Validation TRR, objective evidence must be documented for all requirements (User Needs & RCMs) executed in the Test Management Tool.  Requirement to be validated o Describes an action or set of actions performed to set up a scenario o Describes expected behavior via clearly defined acceptance criteria  Observed Test Results o Actual results describe observations that do not have to be interpreted. The data is documented in a manner that allows re-creation of the results. The data documented allows the reader to come to the same conclusion as the tester.  Objective Evidence o The documented evidence for the test case provides sufficient data to prove what happened, when, by whom and how. o The objective evidence proves the system is functioning or performing as designed or intended. o Objective evidence must be captured for all User Needs and RCM Acceptance Criteria associated to the test case to justify the validation goal has been met. o

sub_query: How is risk integrated into design and development activities?
source: D56979 HAL 4.0 Risk Management Report rev07.pdf
title: 4.0 Review of Risk Management Activities > 4.1 Implementation of the risk management plan
text_content: A comprehensive risk study has been conducted for HAL 4.0 in accordance with ISO 14971:2019 (1) and Varian’s Risk Management Process. The study was conducted by cross-functional teams consisting of the members identified in Table 1. The teams reviewed risk to health posed by all persons interacting with HAL 4.0. This analysis considered hazards that could occur both in normal use (or foreseeable misuse) and hazards associated with potential system or sub-system failures. Hazards, hazardous situations, and harms have been identified using SHaF (System Hazard Framework). The hazards, hazardous situations, and harms, applicable to HAL 4.0 are documented in HAL 4.0 Elaboration Phase RHA (10). The risks associated with these hazards and hazardous situations have been assessed and risk control measures have been identified. The risks associated with these hazards and hazardous situations as well as the risk control measures are documented in this report in Appendix B: Risk Assessment.

text_content: Varian implements a product lifecycle framework that guides the incorporation of the Secure by Design principle in Varian products and solutions. The lifecycle framework also addresses the continuous management of cybersecurity risks after the products or solutions are released to the field. As part of the post-market cybersecurity maintenance, cybersecurity risk assessments are conducted periodically, or whenever it is needed in response to post-market cybersecurity signals such as 3rd party component vulnerability disclosure (e.g., critical Microsoft vulnerability CVE- 2019-0708).  Depending on the nature and the level of the risks, the results of the post-market cybersecurity risk assessments can lead to product updates, bug fixes, field services, Product Advisory, or Product Security Notification. Further, Varian control software is designed to constantly check machine operation such that treatment or motion is stopped immediately should a failure occur (fail safe). Machine movements can adversely affect mechanical structures and cabling.  Varian systems are designed to ensure that structural components have infinite fatigue life. Cable windups are designed to be highly reliable and thoroughly tested to ensure that protective earth conductors are not adversely compromised. Because the use environment temperature is carefully controlled, thermal fatigue is considered only for high operating temperature components such as the X-ray target. Listed below are the main components (main assemblies) affected by the Lifetime Limiting Factors.  Also identified is whether a failure of the component represents a single fault condition affecting the safety of the patient or operator.  Corresponding lifetime tests and/or analysis are shown.

Harm: Physical injury or damage to the health of people, or damage to property or the environment.5 Integrity: Data, information and software are accurate and complete (i.e., the assurance that these items have not been improperly modified.)3 Lifecycle: All phases in the life of a medical device, from initial device definition through design completion, release to manufacturing, delivery/installation for users, servicing and maintenance, and end of support processes. Malware: Software designed with malicious intent to disrupt normal function, gather sensitive information, and/or access other connected systems.5 Privileged User: User who is authorized to perform security-relevant functions relating to the medical device software that ordinary users are not authorized to perform. Risk: Combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm.5

title: 1. Basic Information > 2.2 Interoperation Maintenance
text_content: Support & Maintenance Process Customers and any employee that experience erroneous behavior are advised to report these errors to the business unit that has sold the TPS system. These reports are analyzed by the respective complaint handling department. Every error is inserted into the corrective and preventive action (CAPA) and complaint handling procedures and, where necessary, is reproduced in the laboratory for analysis. The complaint handling and the CAPA procedures ensure that the complaints are assessed for the need of Medical Device Reporting (MDR). Also ensured are a root cause analysis of the erroneous behavior and the provision of appropriate change requests for fixing the error. Depending on the significance of the problem, required changes are made by returning to the appropriate development state and implementing appropriate fixes. The changes are then retested and re-evaluated and are either distributed as updates through standard service

title: 1. Introduction > 1.9 Manufacturing Plan Guidance
text_content: The Manufacturing NPI Project Manager is accountable for downloading the Manufacturing Plan template, completing product development team level information (Product config, Scope, etc.) and maintaining the Manufacturing Plan. The Manufacturing NPI Project Manager is ultimately the lead on all manufacturing deliverables/activities. The Manufacturing NPI Project Manager will collect and collate content for the Manufacturing Operations Plan from across the entire project environment and the applicable organizational environments. As decisions are made regarding how the product should be implemented in manufacturing, a version of the Manufacturing Plan should be updated to reflect those changes. A new version should be released as determined in previous recommendation. By Design Freeze Review the Manufacturing Plan should, at a minimum, address every deliverable in the Design Transfer Checklist for which Manufacturing Operations is responsible.

title: 4. HAL 4.0 Test Case Selection Rationale (Continued)
text_content: Regression testing is initially based on new, modified, or impacted user needs and human operated RCMs. A subset would typically be performed for minor releases only. Impact analysis is not required if all user needs are planned to be executed.  An impact analysis shall be required, and justification shall be provided, for any user needs or human operated RCMs that shall not be validated in this release. Any test case not executed, including during subsequent test rounds, will be justified in this section of the document. For any user need or human operated RCM justified as not validated in this release, previous test results in ADLM ADO are considered valid.

text_content: Claims are organized by ID numbers that can be referenced in future marketing material. Each claim should provide evidence that is adequate to substantiate the claim, often in the form of technical documentation or study references, appearing below the individual claim. Each subsection is intended to be version-specific. For example, subsection 3.1 would include claims relating to the initial version of the product, while subsection 3.2 would include claims relating to the subsequent version and so on. Claims from previous versions, if still valid for the latest version, can be pulled into the latest version by referencing the Claim ID.

Upgrades and Conversions: Upgrades from a previous version to HAL 4.0 can be made in some countries where HAL 4.0 is cleared. The details of which countries allow this will be detailed later in the project. For China, the system updates cannot engage any re-labelling activities, the information on device label should not be changed, such as manufacturing date, model name, NMPA license number etc. . Conversions from Halcyon to Ethos radiotherapy sytem can be made in some countries. Conversions involve changes to the covers, installing and enabling the Ethos TPS (On-couch session manager or OSM) function and changing the nameplate to include “Ethos Radiotherapy System” while manufacturing date and serial number remain the same. text_content: The system build versioning follows the product versioning instructions L6954 – Product Versioning. This versioning scheme is primarily used for formal builds generated for test readiness review. XX . YY . Zz BB • XX = Two numbers indicating major release • YY = Two numbers indicating minor release • Zz  = Two numbers indicating maintenance release • BB = Two numbers indicating engineering build number where o Left side ‘B’ is used for TRR number o Right side ‘B’ is used for build iteration within a given TRR number. source: D67423 HAL 4.0 Product Technical Requirements Rev 02.pdf
title: 1.  Product Model > 1.4 Version Naming Rule
text_content: Version naming rule: MA.MI.B.RE MA stands for “Major”, MI for “Minor”, B for “Build” and RE for “Revision”. Major: This is a given number defined by engineering at project start, which will not change after project start. Typically, the number is increased when the project defines major changes to the previous release. Minor: This is a given number defined by engineering at project start, which will not be changed after project start. Typically, the number is increased when the project defines minor changes to the previous release. Build: The build number shall be increased on each released build of an application/component, independent from internal and/or external use of the application/component. The number starts at 0 with a new project and increments by 1 with each released build. Revision: Listing this number is optional. The purpose of this number is under the responsibility of the project. It can be used to update a particular build when the Build number cannot be changed.

source: D65887 HAL4.0 IEC 60601-2-1 (2020) 201.10.101.1.1.5 - TERMINATION OF IRRADIATION by DOSE MONITORING SYSTEM Rev 01.docx.pdf
title: 4. IEC Compliance Checks
text_content: Compliance is checked as follows: a) b) c) e) TYPE TEST grade A – Statement regarding the means used to ensure compliance. Ref a) and c) - Grade A Type Tests – Design Statement The ability of the secondary channel, that is the BGM FPGA level, to terminate irradiation is verified between treatments as part of the dosimetry test during the mode (radiation type and energy) selection process which is called the CalCheck cycle. The CalCheck cycle includes a first check that the continuity of the cables for all ion chamber plate signals is verified. This continuity check is achieved by introducing a step change in the ion chamber power supply voltage level and then detecting the corresponding induced charges in the ion chamber collection plates. This is needed because later the dosimetry check is performed locally on the BGM-CONT only. Such a test is valid because passing the continuity test can prove that the cable connection and the connections through the ion chamber, are all intact.

title: 9. The patient is unloaded. > 2.5.3.1 Control System Safety Infrastructure > 2.5.3.1.3 Version Reporting and Compatibility Check > 2.5.3.1.3.2 Compatibility Check among Software and Hardware
text_content: All software and FPGA versions, and the PCB part identifiers are fixed for a certain build, while the PCB version (dash number and revision) can vary between system to system. A subsystem is responsible for checking the compatibility of itself and its software and FPGA components. It is also responsible for checking the compatibility between its components, such as FPGA to PCB or PCB to PCB. Supervisor specifies a list of compatible versions for each Node in its configuration data item tree. It compares the Node version received from the Node with the compatibility list and raises an active fault if the received version is not found in the list. A Node does not cross check the Supervisor version since the compatibility check by the Supervisor is considered sufficient. Date D60152 02 Date of last e-sign Page 175 of 272 DND06T03 Rev 01 detection algorithm matches the HAL 1 XI implementation (for one floodfield): • The (offset corrected) floodfield image is subdivided into vertical stripes of 128 pixels to avoid readout module boundary discontinuities. • Within every stripe a 5x5 median filter is applied. The filter size is reduced accordingly towards the borders of the stripes. • The (offset corrected) floodfield image is gain corrected with the filtered image. • The mean absolute deviation of the corrected image is calculated. • Pixels with absolute deviation greater than 15 times the mean absolute deviation are marked as defect.
